nodes.summary: Job that publishes the built manual artifact to the github-pages environment via actions/deploy-pages, depending on the build job.

nodes.name: sync-to-clean.yml

nodes.summary: Job that removes excluded internal files, commits the filtered tree, and force-pushes to the clean public repository, also forwarding version tags.

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Start with the README to understand what miStudio is: an end-to-end mechanistic interpretability platform that walks a model through dataset ingestion, sparse autoencoder (SAE) training, feature extraction, LLM labeling, and model steering. It lays out the architecture, tech stack, and how the pieces fit together, giving you the map for the rest of this tour. CLAUDE.md complements it with the day-to-day development workflow and service topology.

tour.title: Application Entry Points
tour.description: Two processes boot the system. backend/src/main.py constructs the FastAPI app, wires the API router, CORS, and lifecycle hooks, and is the root of the backend dependency graph. frontend/src/main.tsx mounts the React application that renders the miStudio interface. Seeing both together shows the client/server split you'll trace through the rest of the tour.

tour.title: Core Foundations: Config & Database
tour.description: These are the two most depended-upon files in the entire backend (fan-in 41 and 48). config.py centralizes environment-driven settings — data directories, database URLs, model interval timers — while database.py sets up the async SQLAlchemy engine and session factory that every service and worker imports. Almost everything downstream reads from here, so understanding them early demystifies later modules.
tour.languageLesson: SQLAlchemy's async engine uses asyncpg under the hood; sessions are provided to FastAPI routes via dependency injection (get_db) and to Celery tasks via a separate synchronous session factory, since Celery workers run outside the asyncio event loop.

tour.title: The Data Model
tour.description: The domain model defines miStudio's vocabulary. model.py, dataset.py, training.py, and feature.py are among the highest fan-in files because nearly every service and API endpoint reads or writes these SQLAlchemy ORM classes. They map directly to the pipeline stages: a Dataset feeds a Training run that produces an SAE, whose Features get extracted and labeled. Learn these entities and the rest of the code reads as operations on them.
tour.languageLesson: PostgreSQL JSONB columns store flexible metadata (hyperparameters, config snapshots) alongside typed columns, letting the schema stay stable while feature configs evolve.

tour.title: Database Migrations & Schema Evolution
tour.description: The Alembic migration files are the source of truth for the physical schema. Each version file adds a table that backs a model from Step 4 — training_metrics for time-series loss curves, external_saes for imported autoencoders, and the enhanced/neuronpedia tables that support labeling and export. Reading them shows how the schema grew alongside the features described in this tour.
tour.languageLesson: Alembic migrations should be ordered and reversible. Each file declares upgrade()/downgrade() so the database can move forward or roll back one incremental step at a time, keeping schema changes in lockstep with the ORM models.

tour.title: Pipeline Stage 1 — Dataset Ingestion
tour.description: The pipeline begins with data. dataset_service.py handles HuggingFace and local dataset ingestion, while dataset_tokenization_service.py prepares raw text into tokenized activations-ready form. Because this is long-running work, dataset_tasks.py runs it as a Celery job and streams progress over WebSocket. This is the first concrete example of the service + worker pattern repeated across every pipeline stage.

tour.title: Pipeline Stage 2 — SAE Training (ML Core)
tour.description: This is the heart of the platform. sae_architectures.py defines the six paper-grounded SAE frameworks (Standard, TopK, JumpReLU, and more), sparse_autoencoder.py implements the model modules, and sae_training.py runs the optimization loop with sparsity penalties and dead-neuron detection. training_service.py orchestrates the job and training_tasks.py executes it on the GPU via Celery, emitting live loss metrics.
tour.languageLesson: PyTorch SAEs use a straight-through estimator (STE) for the JumpReLU L0 sparsity term: the count of active features is non-differentiable, so a sigmoid surrogate carries the gradient while the forward pass uses the hard threshold.

tour.title: Pipeline Stage 3 — Feature Extraction
tour.description: Once an SAE is trained, extraction discovers what each latent feature responds to. layer_discovery.py introspects any transformer architecture dynamically (no hardcoded whitelists), forward_hooks.py taps the target layer's activations, and extraction_service.py runs the dataset through the model+SAE to collect top-activating examples. extraction_tasks.py drives this as a monitored Celery job.
tour.languageLesson: PyTorch forward hooks attach to a module and capture its output tensor during the forward pass without modifying the model definition — the mechanism that makes architecture-agnostic activation capture possible.

tour.title: Pipeline Stage 4 — LLM Feature Labeling
tour.description: Extracted features need human-readable names. enhanced_labeling_service.py implements two-pass labeling — parallel per-example summarization followed by synthesis — using openai_labeling_service.py, which standardizes on the official OpenAI SDK and auto-detects reasoning-class models. enhanced_labeling_tasks.py runs the batch asynchronously and streams progress so the Feature Detail UI updates live.

tour.title: Pipeline Stage 5 — Model Steering
tour.description: The final stage turns interpretation into intervention. steering_service.py injects trained SAE features into a model's forward pass to steer generation, using the same dynamic layer discovery from Step 8 so any transformer works without code changes. steering_tasks.py isolates this GPU work in a Celery task with zombie-process detection, closing the loop from raw data to controllable model behavior.

tour.title: The Task Queue & Real-time Backbone
tour.description: Every pipeline stage you just saw runs on the same infrastructure. celery_app.py configures the Celery app, routing, and Beat schedule; base_task.py provides the shared task base with DB sessions and error handling; and websocket_emitter.py (fan-in 20) is the single choke point through which all workers push progress, metrics, and completion events. This is the connective tissue that makes long-running ML jobs feel interactive.

tour.title: The Frontend Real-time Client
tour.description: On the browser side, WebSocketContext.tsx (the most depended-upon frontend module, fan-in 19) manages the Socket.IO connection and channel subscriptions, while client.ts is the shared axios HTTP client every store uses. Together they mirror the backend's WebSocket-first pattern: React hooks subscribe to job channels, update Zustand stores, and fall back to HTTP polling when the socket drops — the same emitter events from Step 11 land here.
tour.languageLesson: React Context provides a single shared Socket.IO instance to the whole component tree; hooks subscribe to entity-scoped rooms (e.g. training/{id}) on mount and unsubscribe on unmount to avoid leaking listeners.

tour.title: Containerization & Local Orchestration
tour.description: Building on the services you've traced, this shows how they're packaged and run together. backend/Dockerfile builds the Python/ML image, and docker-compose.yml orchestrates the full stack — PostgreSQL, Redis, backend, the Celery worker and Beat scheduler, frontend, and nginx — as the services that host every pipeline stage. This is the concrete deployment of the architecture from Step 1.
tour.languageLesson: docker-compose uses depends_on and health checks to order service startup (Postgres/Redis before backend and Celery), and named volumes to persist database data and model files across container restarts.

tour.title: CI/CD & Production Deployment
tour.description: The capstone ties the codebase to how it ships. docker-images.yml is the GitHub Actions pipeline that builds and publishes the backend and frontend images on every push to main, and k8s/mistudio-deployment.yaml deploys them to the production Kubernetes cluster (RTX 3090 GPU node). This closes the development loop: commit → CI build → k8s deploy, exactly the workflow described in the project README.
tour.languageLesson: GitHub Actions jobs run in parallel with sequential steps; caching dependency layers and using build matrices speeds up the multi-image (backend + frontend) build. Kubernetes Deployments manage GPU-pinned pod replicas with rolling updates.
